title: Cat Grit in Urine: Causes, Risks, and Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Find out what sand-like crystals in your cat's urine mean. Learn why this medium-urgency issue requires a vet visit and how photos help triage.
content_html:
What is Grit in Cat Urine?

Finding sand-like particles or grit in your cat's urine is a sign of crystalluria. These are microscopic minerals, most commonly struvite or calcium oxalate, that have precipitated out of the urine and clumped together. This condition is often a component of Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD) and can cause significant inflammation and pain in the bladder and urethra.

Is This an Emergency?

Urgency Level: Medium. If your cat is still urinating successfully, this is a medium-urgency situation that requires a veterinary appointment within 24 hours. However, if your cat is straining without producing urine, crying out in the litter box, or excessively grooming their genital area, this may indicate a life-threatening urinary blockage. In the case of a suspected blockage, seek emergency veterinary care immediately.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear photo of the crystals and the surrounding urine is incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. A photo allows the vet to assess the quantity of the grit, the presence of blood (hematuria), and the overall concentration of the urine. This visual evidence helps them determine the severity of the irritation and prioritize your cat's case during the intake process.

faqs.question: Can I treat urinary crystals at home with a diet change?
faqs.answer: While diet is part of the long-term solution, you should never attempt to treat crystals at home without a diagnosis. A vet must determine the type of crystal to prescribe the correct therapeutic food; the wrong diet can actually make some types of crystals worse.

faqs.question: Is my cat in pain if I see grit in their urine?
faqs.answer: Yes, passing crystals is often very uncomfortable for cats. It can feel like passing sandpaper through a very sensitive area, leading to inflammation and a constant urge to urinate.

faqs.question: Which cats are most at risk for urinary grit?
faqs.answer: While any cat can develop crystals, neutered male cats are at the highest risk for life-threatening complications because their urethras are narrower and more easily blocked by the grit.
